title: What is Self-Supervised Learning?
concept: Self-supervised learning trains a model by turning structure already present in raw data into prediction tasks, so it can learn useful representations without humans manually labeling every example.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main goal of a good embedding?

options.text: To place similar items near each other in a meaningful representation space.
To replace training data with random numbers.
To guarantee that every input gets a unique label from a human.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Embeddings try to capture useful structure, such as similarity, in a form a model can reuse.

cards.title: A library with no sticky notes
cards.body: Imagine owning a giant library full of books, but none of them are tagged by topic. That sounds messy for supervised learning. Self-supervised learning asks a smarter question: can the model learn from the patterns already inside the books before anyone adds labels?

cards.title: What is self-supervised learning?
cards.body: Self-supervised learning is a training approach where the model creates its own learning signal from raw data. Instead of a human providing targets like "cat" or "spam", the model predicts missing, future, or rearranged parts of the input and learns useful internal representations from that process.

cards.title: Learning by solving built-in puzzles
cards.body: It is like giving yourself practice questions by covering parts of a page and trying to fill them back in. The page already contains the answer somewhere — you are using the material itself to create the challenge.

cards.title: Hide a word, predict the word
cards.body: Suppose a sentence says, "The chef sliced the ___ with a knife." If the model must guess the missing word, it has to learn something about language, context, and meaning. No human had to write a separate label — the hidden word was already in the data.

cards.title: It is not label-free chaos
cards.body: A common myth is that self-supervised learning means training with no targets at all. Not quite. It still uses targets, but the targets are derived from the data itself instead of being manually attached by people.

cards.title: Manual labels vs. built-in structure
cards.body: Both approaches teach a model, but they get their targets from different places.

left.text:
Humans attach labels
like cat, dog, or fraud
Strong but expensive signal

right.text:
Data creates its own targets
like missing words or future steps
Scales with raw data

options.text: Hide words in raw sentences and train the model to predict the hidden words.
Pay people to label every photo with one of 20 classes.
Skip training and use random weights forever.
Delete parts of the dataset so the model has less to learn from.

exercises.explain: Correct. The missing word comes from the original sentence, so the data itself provides the target.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Self-supervised learning still uses targets, but those targets can come from the raw data itself.
exercises.explain: True. That is the central idea: generate supervision from structure that is already present in the input.

exercises.sentence: Self-supervised learning looks for training signals inside the ___ data.
exercises.explain: Raw is correct. The model learns from the structure already present in raw text, images, audio, or other inputs.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best matches self-supervised learning?

options.text: A model sees the first few seconds of sensor data and predicts what likely comes next.
A teacher grades every sample by hand before training starts.
An engineer hard-codes every possible answer into the model weights.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Predicting the next step uses structure already present in the sequence.

exercises.prompt: A team has billions of raw documents but only a tiny set of hand-labeled ones. Why is self-supervised learning such a strong first move?

options.text: Because the model can learn general language patterns from the raw documents by creating prediction tasks from the documents themselves.
Because self-supervised learning automatically turns every task into reinforcement learning.
Because the model no longer needs any objective or target during training.
Because human labels become less accurate when the dataset gets large.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Self-supervised learning lets the model use huge raw corpora to build useful representations before small labeled sets are used for specific tasks.
